text: While catalytic borylations of sp³ C–H bonds typically favor primary positions or electronically activated secondary sites, functionalization at tertiary C–H bonds has remained an elusive challenge. In this work, we report a versatile iridium-catalyzed method for the bridgehead borylation of bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes and (hetero)bicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes. This transformation is highly site-selective for the tertiary C–H bond and demonstrates broad functional group tolerance across more than 35 examples. We show that the protocol is effective for both the late-stage diversification of complex pharmaceuticals and the streamlined synthesis of novel bicyclic building blocks. Mechanistic investigations, including kinetic analysis and computational modeling, indicate that C–H bond cleavage occurs with a relatively low energy barrier. Instead, the turnover-limiting step is identified as an isomerization process required prior to the C–B bond-forming reductive elimination.
